elements.sent-2411-001: Vortex information of scalar pair production in circularly polarized field is investigated in the multiphoton regime.
elements.sent-2411-002: We find that vortex orientation is related to the intrinsic orbital angular momentum of created particles associating with the helicity of absorbed photons, while the magnitude of the orbital angular momentum, i.e., the topology charge is determined by the number of absorbed photons.
elements.sent-2411-003: Moreover, the properties of particle creation and vortices formation can be understood by analyzing the pair production process in quasiparticle representation.
elements.sent-2411-004: This study provides new insights into the angular momentum transfer from field to particle in the scalar pair production process.
elements.sent-2411-005: It is expected that there are similar findings about vortex features for different spin alignment in electron-positron pair production in strong fields via the topology charge as a new freedom.
elements.sent-2411-006: The phenomenon of electron-positron pair production in vacuum~\cite{Heisenberg:1936nmg,Sauter:1931zz,Schwinger:1951nm,PhysRevD.44.1825,Fradkin:1991,Xie:2017xoj,DiPiazza:2011tq,Ruf:2008ahs is closely analogous to ionization in atomic physics~\cite{Keldysh:1965ojf,Ma:2013,Troup:1972xkz,NBDelone:1994,RKopold:2002 .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Both processes are including above-threshold ionization, harmonic generation in perturbative multiphoton process~\cite{BECKER200235,Michael:2005,Muller:2009zzf,Aleksandrov:2021ylw and the non-perturbative tunneling process~\cite{Schwinger:1951nm,Xie:2017xoj,Klaiber:023,Klaiber:2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Besides, pairs production and ionization driven by varying dynamic fields exhibit many similar effects in momentum space, including a series of multiphoton peaks in single circularly polarized field~\cite{Kohlfurst:2018kxg,Blinne:2013via,Li_2015,Fillion-Gourdeau:2017uss,Geng2020VortexSI , and spiral structures in two counter-rotating electric field with time delay~\cite{Ngoko:2015,Pengel:2017,Li:2017qwd,Li:2018hzi,Li:2019rex,Majczak:2022xlv,Hu:2023pmz,Yusoff:2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Wavefield phase front singularities, vortices, and associated non-integral phases have been introduced and studied in many academic papers~\cite{PMDirac:1931,Aharonov,berry,Allen:1992zz,Beth:1936zz,Bialynicki-Birula:2016unl,Hebenstreit:2016xhn .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In the study of the photodetachment, phase vortices naturally appear in the electronic eigenstates of atoms, resulting in vortex structures in momentum space~\cite{BialynickiBirula1992TheoryOQ .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent research has found that vortex structures also appear in pairs production via the Sauter-Schwinger process~\cite{Bechler:2023kjx,Majczak:2024hmt .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, in the perturbative multiphoton regime, the created particles exhibit distinct angular momentum features that are inherited from the photon helicity~\cite{Kohlfurst:2018kxg,Li_2015,Fan:2024vqi .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This suggests that the angular momentum of the created particles possesses intrinsic properties that may result in singularities in the amplitude phase, e.g., the vortices of the particle carry a quantized orbital angular momentum~\cite{Bialynicki-Birula:2016unl,Lloyd:2017,Bliokh:2017uvr .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This motivates our interest in studying vortices in pairs production in multiphoton-dominated processes under circularly polarized fields.
elements.sent-2411-015: For simplicity, we will consider relativistic scalar particle without spin.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is because that the wave packets with orbital angular momentum (OAM) exhibit fundamental topological and dynamical characteristics that have traditionally been associated primarily with spin~\cite{Bliokh:2007ec .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In this work, the particle distribution function in momentum space is obtained by solving the Klein-Gordon equation and can be further reduced to a two-level system.
elements.sent-2411-018: We find that the vortex structures and the topological charge magnitude are determined by the absorbed photons.
elements.sent-2411-019: Additionally, the evolution of vortices is investigated in the quasiparticle representation, which shows that momentum spectra not only exhibit the spiral structure but also the phase distribution shows the transition from the spiral pattern to the multi-vortex structures.
elements.sent-2411-020: It should be noted that the natural units are employed throughout this paper with $\hbar = c = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-021: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: In Sec.~\ref{Sec:II , the external field and particle distribution function are introduced, while the description of the vortex is reviewed.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Sec.~\ref{Sec:Iv , the numerical results are presented.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Finally, a brief summary is given in Sec.~\ref{summary .}
elements.sent-2411-025: We adopt the following idealized model of circularly polarized electric field: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:1} E\left(t\right) = E\sin^4\left({\omega t}/{2N}\right) \left[ \cos \left(\omega t\right) e_x + \delta \sin\left(\omega t\right) e_y \right],\end{equation} where $E$ gives the external field amplitude in units of the critical field $E_\text{cr = m^2/e$, $\omega$ is the frequency, $N$ is the number of cycles, and $ 0\leq t \leq 2N\pi/\omega$.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:1} E\left(t\right) = E\sin^4\left({\omega t}/{2N}\right) \left[ \cos \left(\omega t\right) e_x + \delta \sin\left(\omega t\right) e_y \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-026: The polarization vector $\boldsymbol{e_x $ and $\boldsymbol{e_y $ are along the $x$ and $y$ directions, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The laser pulse propagates along the $\boldsymbol{e_z}$ axis, with $\lvert \delta \rvert$ representing the ellipticity, constrained by $-1 \leq \delta \leq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-028: For linear polarization, $\delta = 0$, while circular polarization corresponds to $\lvert \delta \rvert = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The sign of $\delta$ determines the helicity, where $\delta = +1$ indicates right-handed circular polarization, and $\delta = -1$ indicates left-handed circular polarization.
elements.sent-2411-030: When we neglect the backreaction, we can solve the Klein-Gordon equation for the scalar pairs production in the background electric field $\boldsymbol{E}\left(t\right)$ in Eq.~\eqref{Eq:1 , along with the associated gauge potential $\boldsymbol{A}\left(t\right)$, and $\boldsymbol E\left(t\right) = -\dot{\boldsymbol A}\left(t\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: A natural formalism for this analysis is provided by Bogoliubov transformations~\cite{Kluger:1998bm .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Given the initial creation and annihilation operators $a_{\boldsymbol{p , b_{\boldsymbol{p ^{\dagger}$, the Bogoliubov transformation coefficients $\alpha_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right), \beta_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right)$ relate these operators to their time-dependent forms $\tilde a_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right),\tilde b_{-{\boldsymbol{p ^{\dagger}\left(t\right)$~\cite{Dumlu:2011rr,Akkermans:2011yn,Dunne:2022zlx \begin{equation} \tilde a_{p}\left(t\right) \tilde b_{-p}^{\dagger}\left(t\right)  =  \alpha_{p}\left(t\right) \beta_{p}^{*}\left(t\right) \beta_{p}\left(t\right) \alpha_{p}^{*}\left(t\right)   a_{p} b_{-p}^{\dagger}  \ ,\end{equation} where $\left| \beta_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right) \right|^2$ is the density of the created particles with canonical momentum $\boldsymbol{p}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The time-dependent Bogoliubov coefficients satisfy the following relations~\cite{Li:2019rex : \begin{equation}\frac{d\alpha_{p}\left(t\right)}{dt} = -\Omega_{p}\left(t\right) \beta_{p}\left(t\right) e^{2i\int^t d \tau \omega_{p}(\tau)}, \notag \frac{d\beta_{p}\left(t\right)}{dt} = -\Omega_{p}\left(t\right) \alpha_{p}\left(t\right) e^{-2i\int^t d \tau\omega_{p}(\tau)},\end{equation} where $\Omega_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right) = - {\dot{\omega _{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right) /{2\omega_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right)}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The time evolution of the system can be directly expressed in terms of the Bogoliubov coefficients, whose dynamics are described by a two-level system.
elements.sent-2411-036: By setting $c_{\alpha, {\boldsymbol{p =e^{-i\int^t d \tau \omega_{\boldsymbol{p (\tau)}\alpha_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right)$, $c_{\beta, {\boldsymbol{p = e^{i\int^t d \tau \omega_{\boldsymbol{p (\tau)}\beta_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right) $, the evolution equation takes the form~\cite{Dunne:2022zlx \begin{equation}i\frac{d}{d t}  c_{\alpha, {p}}\left(t\right) c_{\beta, {p}}\left(t\right)  =  \omega_{p}\left(t\right) -i\Omega_{p}\left(t\right) -i \Omega_{p}\left(t\right) -\omega_{p}\left(t\right)   c_{\alpha, {p}} c_{\beta, {p}} \label{eq:twolevelsys},\end{equation} where $\omega_{\boldsymbol{p }\left(t\right)=\sqrt{m^2 +\boldsymbol q^2\left(t\right) = \sqrt{m^2 + \left[\boldsymbol{p - e\boldsymbol{A}\left(t\right)\right]^2}$ denotes the single-particle energy with kinetic momentum $\boldsymbol {q}\left(t\right)$ given by $\boldsymbol{q}\left(t\right) = \boldsymbol{p - e\boldsymbol{A}\left(t\right)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The single-particle distribution function in momentum space is expressed as \begin{equation}\label{distribution} f({p}) = \lvert c_{\beta, {p}} \rvert ^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-038: It can be verified that the distribution function can be reduced to the well-known quantum Vlasov equation (QVE)~\cite{Blaschke:2017igl,Blaschke:2014fca \begin{equation}\label{Eq:6} \frac{d}{d t} f\left(p, t\right) = \frac{1}{2} \Omega_{p}\left(t\right) \int_{-\infty}^{t} d t^{\prime} \Omega_{p}\left( t^{\prime}\right)\Bigg\{\left[1+2 f\left(p, t^{\prime}\right)\right] \times \notag \cos \left[2 \Theta\left(p, t^{\prime}, t\right)\right]\Bigg\},\end{equation} where $ \Theta\left(\boldsymbol{p , t^{\prime , t\right) = \int_{t^{\prime ^{t d \tau \omega_{\boldsymbol{p }\left( \tau \right) $.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:6} \frac{d}{d t} f\left(p, t\right) = \frac{1}{2} \Omega_{p}\left(t\right) \int_{-\infty}^{t} d t^{\prime} \Omega_{p}\left( t^{\prime}\right)\Bigg\{\left[1+2 f\left(p, t^{\prime}\right)\right] \times \notag \cos \left[2 \Theta\left(p, t^{\prime}, t\right)\right]\Bigg\},\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-046: The integral along an arbitrary closed curve $\mathcal{C}$ around a singularity of the phase satisfies the following quantized condition \begin{equation}\label{curvature} \int_C {A}\cdot dp = 2\pi l.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: In optics, the topological charge $l$ means of intrinsic orbital angular momentum (IOAM)~\cite{Lloyd:2017,Bliokh:2017uvr .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The semiclassical analysis for particle packets also shows that the IOAM of a particle is closely related to the Berry connection~\cite{Bliokh:2007ec,Bliokh:2012az .}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this investigation, taking the primary absorption of photon for example, we find that the number of photons absorbed by the created particles can be presented in the phase.

elements.sent-2411-052: From Fig.~\ref{Fig:1 we can find that the shape of momentum spectrum as well as the phase are similar to the intensity spectrum and phase of the Laguerre-Gaussian beam~\cite{Allen:1992zz,Zhang:2024ofv .}}

elements.sent-2411-055: Most notably, the radius of the absorption ring on the momentum spectrum increases as the number of absorbed photons increases, which is consistent with the phenomenon that large topological charge in the Laguerre-Gaussian mode correspond to large bright ring.

elements.sent-2411-072: Quantum mechanics' superposition principle requires that the wave function of the created particles be a superposition of various possible wave packets with angular momentum modes~\cite{Bliokh:2007ec,Bialynicki-Birula:2016unl .}

elements.sent-2411-085: The relationship between the photon number and the topological charge shows that when the created particles also have $z$-direction momentum $p_z \neq 0$, leading to the larger $p_r$, the number of absorbed photons may increase, and thus large IOAM particles can be obtained.
elements.sent-2411-086: For example, when the particles propagate along $z$-direction with $p_z = 0.8m$, the number of absorbed photons increase, leading to an increase in the number of topological charges as well, i.e., $l\to l+1$.
elements.sent-2411-087: This property gives us two methods of obtaining large IOAM.
elements.sent-2411-088: Either particles with different topological charges can be obtained at different particles' ejected directions for a constant $p_z$, or particles with large topological charges can be captured in the direction along the pulse propagation when we focus on the strongest $n$-photon ring.
elements.sent-2411-089: More interestingly, a simple relationship for $s = 0$ between topological charge and external field frequency can also be easily generalized as follows: $l \propto \omega^{-1}$ or specifically $l = \lceil 2m^*/\omega \rceil$, which is an inevitable result for the topological charge to be IOAM, see Fig.~\ref{Fig:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: For a better understanding of the vortex structures, let us investigate the evolution of the particle and topological charge in the quasiparticle representation.

elements.sent-2411-119: Interestingly, during the period from $t=174.8m^{-1}$ to $t_1$, the QPAP state becomes unstable and the spiral structure in momentum spectra appears in Fig.~\ref{Fig:4 (c).}

elements.sent-2411-123: The vacuum fluctuations cause the spiral arms in the momentum spectra to elongate and rapidly converge to a ring structure.
elements.sent-2411-124: Multiple topological charges also manifest in the phase spectra.

elements.sent-2411-126: The transient process fully enters the RPAP region, and the shape of momentum spectra and the vortex structures are the same as in Figs.~\ref{Fig:2 (a) and (c).}
elements.sent-2411-127: In this study, we numerically investigate the vortex structure induced by rotating electric fields, as well as the evolution of the vortices and momentum spectra in the quasiparticle representation.
elements.sent-2411-128: The vortex direction of the final particle is determined by the polarization of the field, suggesting that the topological charge associated with the vortex reflects the IOAM properties of the particle.
elements.sent-2411-129: The vortex serves as a form of the IOAM of the particle, which is transferred from the photons of the external field.
elements.sent-2411-130: The transfer of angular momentum from $n$ photons to the pairs results in a vortex or topological charge of the particle equal to $n$.
elements.sent-2411-131: The study shows that both the structure and magnitude of the IOAM can be controlled by the external field.
elements.sent-2411-132: In the quasiparticle representation, we also study the time evolution of the vortex.
elements.sent-2411-133: It is found that the vortex structure remains stable during the QPAP stage.
elements.sent-2411-134: In the transient stage, the vacuum rise and fall leads to the instability of the vortex.
elements.sent-2411-135: Once entering the RPAP region, the momentum spectra of the particle and the vortex structure will rapidly converge to the created particle of the final states.
elements.sent-2411-136: This work suggests that the transfer of angular momentum in spinless scalar particles during the multiphoton process can be extend to the angular momentum transfer in multiphoton electron-positron pair production with spin.
elements.sent-2411-137: In spinor QED, however, angular momentum conservation may cause the number of vortices in spin-parallel alignment particles to be different from that in spin-antiparallel alignment.
elements.sent-2411-138: Furthermore, the present study also indicates that the topology charge associated to the vortex as a new freedom provides a potential opportunity to detect or/and control the pair production behaviors.
